summary:
The Adena Culture (c. 500–100 BC) was a pre-Columbian Native American culture of the upper Ohio Valley known for building burial mounds, maintaining trade networks, and producing distinctive artifacts — including the famous Adena Pipe featuring a human figure — representing an early phase of the mound-building tradition that would reach its height in the subsequent [[event:1186|Hopewell Culture]].

Named after the estate in Ohio where their artifacts were first identified, the Adena people lived in small communities practicing a mixed economy of hunting, gathering, and horticulture. Their most visible legacy is their burial mounds — earthen tumuli ranging from small family mounds to large ceremonial structures — which contained elaborately prepared burials with grave goods indicating belief in an afterlife and social differentiation. The Great Serpent Mound of Ohio (though its precise dating is debated) represents the most spectacular Adena or possibly related earthwork — an effigy mound in the shape of an uncoiling serpent over 400 meters long. Adena trade networks brought copper from the Great Lakes region and marine shells from the Atlantic and Gulf coasts. The Adena tradition gradually merged into or was replaced by the Hopewell tradition around 100 BC, though the mound-building practices continued.
